title: Veggie Lo Mein
description: A quick and vibrant Asian-inspired vegetarian noodle dish featuring tender vegetables, savory sauce, and perfectly cooked whole wheat spaghetti. This colorful lo mein bowl combines crisp broccoli, snow peas, shiitake mushrooms, and bell peppers in a delicious tamari-ginger sauce that delivers authentic Chinese flavors in just 20 minutes.
ingredients: 1/3 cup reduced-sodium tamari (reduced-sodium soy sauce, or coconut aminos)
1 tablespoon honey (or pure maple syrup to make it vegan)
1 (3.5-ounce) package shiitake mushrooms (stemmed and thinly sliced)
1 red bell pepper (seeded and thinly sliced)
1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt (plus more to taste)
instructions: Cook the 8 ounces spaghetti in a large pot of salted boiling water until just shy of al dente (about 1-2 minutes less than package directions). Reserve about 1/4 cup of pasta water before draining. Drain the noodles and set aside.
Make the sauce by whisking together 1/3 cup tamari, 1 tablespoon grated ginger, 1 tablespoon honey, 1 tablespoon toasted sesame oil, and 2 teaspoons sriracha in a small bowl. Set aside.

Heat 1 tablespoon olive or avocado oil in a large skillet or wok over medium-high heat until shimmering. Add the shiitake mushrooms, red bell pepper, 1 cup grated carrots, and 3 cloves minced garlic. Cook, stirring frequently, until tender, 3 to 4 minutes.
Add 3 cups broccoli florets and 1 cup snow peas to the skillet. Toss to combine, season with 1/4 teaspoon salt, and cook for another 2 to 3 minutes until broccoli is bright green and crisp-tender.

Add the drained noodles and sauce to the skillet. Toss everything together over medium heat for about 1 minute until the noodles are well coated and heated through. If the mixture seems dry or sticky, splash in a little reserved pasta water. Taste and add more salt if desired.
Divide among 4 bowls and garnish with sliced green onions before serving.
notes: For gluten-free: Use coconut aminos instead of tamari and gluten-free noodles.
Don't skip stemming the shiitake mushrooms - the stems are tough and woody.
Sriracha heat varies by brand - start with 2 teaspoons, then adjust at the end.
For extra protein, add pan-fried tofu cubes or shelled edamame.
